nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing blackwater streams and rivers with rocky substrates, submerged driftwood, and leaf litter; often found sheltering in crevices and under logs in low-light conditions

decorPreferences: driftwood (essential — dietary fiber and grazing surface)
cave-like hiding spots (terracotta tubes, coconut hides, PVC pipe)
smooth or fine-grain substrate (sand or small gravel)
flat rocks and slate for grazing
dense planting optional (Java fern, Anubias, mosses tolerate shading)
moderate to strong current with high oxygenation

diet.notes: The Bushy Nose Pleco feeds primarily by rasping algae and wood, and driftwood should always be present in the tank as a source of dietary fiber. The bulk of its diet should come from algae wafers, spirulina tablets, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, and peas), and raw cucumber slices placed near the substrate at night. It also accepts high-quality sinking pellets and repashy gel foods. Small amounts of protein — such as bloodworm or brine shrimp — can be offered once or twice a week, as the species is herbivore-leaning but does consume biofilm, aufwuchs (the community of microorganisms and algae that grow on submerged surfaces), and occasional invertebrates in the wild. Unlike carnivore-leaning relatives such as Hypancistrus, Ancistrus genuinely rasps algae and is a functional algae-control species.

avoidWith: large aggressive cichlids (oscars, flowerhorn) — pleco suckers may latch on fish
discus and other large slow-moving flat-bodied fish (sucker harassment risk)
very small nano fish where pleco size creates intimidation
salt-sensitive tankmates if salt additive is used

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by JanRehschuh, 2560x1706. Sharp three-quarter view of a mature male on a coconut hide; clearly shows the elaborate branched fleshy 'bushy nose' bristles and dark spotted armored body — the key diagnostic feature.

Photo by Andre Karwath (Aka), 2946x1035. Tack-sharp full lateral view of a bristlenose showing spotting, striped fins and snout bristles. Caveat: labeled 'Ancistrus sp.' at genus level, consistent with the species-complex note in careNotes.
Photo by Guerin Nicolas, 2592x1944. Lateral view of a dolichopterus-labeled specimen showing the classic dark body with pale white spotting; dorsal partly obscured by plant leaf.

summary: The Bushy Nose Pleco, also sold as the Bristlenose Pleco (Ancistrus dolichopterus), is a compact armored catfish that typically reaches 10–15 cm. It is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly plecos available, valued for its algae-grazing habit and the elaborate branched fleshy bristles that develop on the snout of mature males.
careNotes:
The Bushy Nose Pleco is widely regarded as one of the most practical plecos for home aquaria, largely because it stays compact at 10–15 cm — a significant advantage over the common pleco (Pterygoplichthys spp.), which can exceed 45 cm. The most distinctive feature is the male's elaborate fleshy, branched bristles ('bushy nose') that develop on the snout tip and around the upper lip. Females have small, shorter bristles restricted to the snout margin, while juveniles of both sexes are initially indistinguishable.

Driftwood must be present in the tank. The Bushy Nose Pleco rasps wood constantly, gaining both dietary fiber (essential for gut health) and biofilm. Zucchini slices, cucumber rounds, and algae wafers placed near the substrate at night are readily accepted. Despite being sold partly as an algae-control species, this pleco still needs supplemental feeding and will not subsist on tank glass alone.

Cave territories are important to this species' wellbeing: males tend to defend hollows vigorously from rival males and other cave-spawning fish. Providing at least one cave per male — terracotta tubes and coconut hides both work well — helps reduce conflict. Keeping a single male in a community tank is generally the most straightforward approach; two males can coexist in a larger tank (200 L or more) if multiple well-separated caves are available.

The species is sold under the trade names 'Bristlenose' and 'Bushy Nose,' but the Ancistrus genus is a species complex. Wild-caught fish labeled A. dolichopterus are often A. cirrhosus or undescribed hybrids. The true A. dolichopterus is a Rio Negro blackwater fish (pH 4.5–6.5, very soft), but the vast majority of aquarium specimens are multi-generational captive-bred fish that tolerate a wide pH range (5.5–7.5) and moderate hardness without issue. Soft, slightly acidic water is preferred but not required for captive-bred stock.

Water movement should be moderate to strong with high oxygenation — the species originates in well-aerated stream environments and tends to be sensitive to low dissolved oxygen and poor water quality. Regular water changes of around 25–30% weekly are strongly advised; loricariids (armored catfish in general) are susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections when water quality deteriorates. Copper-based medications should be avoided, as with all armored and scaleless catfish.

See also: [common-pleco] (Pterygoplichthys pardalis) — grows to 45+ cm, unsuitable for most home tanks; [clown-pleco] (Panaqolus maccus, L104) — similar cave-dweller but more wood-dependent; [zebra-pleco] (Hypancistrus zebra, L046) — carnivore, specialist care, CITES-restricted.
breedingNotes:
The Bushy Nose Pleco is widely regarded as one of the most readily bred plecos in captivity, which makes it a practical choice for hobbyists who want to raise their own stock. Males select and defend a cave, enticing females inside; spawning occurs within the cave and the male takes exclusive parental responsibility, fanning and guarding the bright orange-yellow egg clutch (30–100+ eggs) for 4–7 days until hatching. Fry cling to the cave wall and absorb their yolk sac over a further 2–4 days before becoming free-swimming. The male continues guarding the fry briefly after they emerge.

Spawning can often be encouraged by conditioning both sexes on a varied, vegetable-rich diet, then performing a slightly larger and slightly cooler water change to simulate rainy-season inflow (a drop of around 1–2°C tends to be effective). The cave should be just large enough for the male to barely turn around inside — too spacious and he may not claim it reliably. Moving the spawning cave to a dedicated raising tank improves fry survival. Fry are herbivorous from the start and accept algae wafers, spirulina powder, and finely grated zucchini. Growth is relatively slow — juveniles typically reach 3–4 cm in approximately 3 months at 26°C.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ancistrus dolichopterus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (23–29°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (36–268 ppm / 2–15 dH), native range (Rio Negro, Brazil), biotope (blackwater, fast-flowing), diet (algae, wood, vegetable matter), cave-breeding behaviour, and species-complex taxonomy note distinguishing wild A. dolichopterus from captive-bred Ancistrus strains.

additional.name: FishBase — Ancistrus dolichopterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 14.0 cm SL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Rio Negro drainage, Brazil), temperature (22–28°C), trophic level (herbivore/detritivore). Confirmed IUCN Least Concern. Species listed under multiple synonyms consistent with Ancistrus species-complex instability.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bristlenose Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for trade-level care guidance: tank size (30 gal / ~114 L minimum), temperature (60–80°F / 15.5–26.7°C — note wide range, captive-bred tolerance), diet emphasis on vegetables and driftwood, beginner suitability, and sexing by bristle morphology. Note: Aquarium Co-Op uses 'Bristlenose Pleco' as a broad trade grouping rather than strictly A. dolichopterus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Ancistrus species (Bristlenose catfish)
additional.notes: Supplemented cave-breeding behaviour details (male parental care, egg clutch size 30–100+, incubation 4–7 days), conspecific male aggression, driftwood dietary requirement, and species-complex taxonomic instability notes. Confirmed pH range 5.5–7.5 and soft-to-moderate hardness preference.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Ancistrus dolichopterus (L183)
additional.notes: L-number trade reference (L183). Confirmed Rio Negro blackwater origin for wild-type, adult size, cave spawning behaviour, and notes on captive-bred versus wild stock pH tolerance. Useful secondary corroboration for loricariid-specific details not covered by mainstream fishkeeping sources.

fish.breedingNotes: The Bushy Nose Pleco is a cave-spawner. The male guards and fans a clutch of 30–100+ eggs for 4–7 days, then continues watching over the fry for a further few days after hatching. Providing a snug cave — one that barely fits the male — and conditioning both fish on a vegetable-rich diet are the most reliable ways to encourage spawning. The species is widely considered one of the most readily bred plecos in captivity and will often spawn without special intervention in a well-maintained tank where caves are present.
